title: Exercise Load Management and Return to Gym
synonyms: Exercise Load Management and Return to Gym

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: This guideline helps physiotherapists progress patients back to training, resistance work, and conditioning without provoking repeated flare cycles or unsafe return.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Patient improving from injury and wanting to resume training
- Load spikes causing repeated flares
- Uncertainty about safe exercise selection, volume, or progression

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Return to gym should be staged and task-specific, not all-or-nothing
- Pain during exercise does not always mean harm, but worsening pain, swelling, or function loss after sessions should change the plan
- Load, recovery, sleep, and technique all affect whether the patient succeeds

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Confirm the injury is stable and there are no unresolved red flags before progression
- Advise temporary substitution or load reduction rather than abrupt total rest where possible
- Escalate if training repeatedly exposes major instability, neurological signs, or uncontrolled pain

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Progress exercise by one main variable at a time such as volume, intensity, speed, or complexity
- Link return milestones to job demands when the worker also needs to remain fit for duty
- Use symptom response over 24 to 48 hours to judge the right next step

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - A return-to-gym plan should be realistic enough that the patient actually follows it outside the clinic
